stem: Find the SMALLER root of the characteristic equation associated with \( y'' + ([[b]])y' + ([[c]])y = 0 \).
solution: The characteristic equation \( x^{2}+([[b]])x+([[c]]) = 0 \) factors with roots \( [[r1]] \) and \( [[r2]] \); the smaller one is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the LARGER root, not the smaller one.
This negates the smaller root instead of reporting it directly.
This reports the coefficient \( b \) instead of a root.
This reports the coefficient \( c \) instead of a root.
This is one more than the correct root.
This is one less than the correct root.

stem: Without solving for the individual roots, use Vieta's formulas to find the SUM of the roots of the characteristic equation of \( y'' + ([[b]])y' + ([[c]])y = 0 \).
solution: For \( x^{2}+bx+c=0 \), Vieta's formulas give sum of roots \( = -b \). Here \( b = [[b]] \), so the sum is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports the product of the roots (which equals \( c \)) instead of the sum.
This negates the product of the roots rather than using \( -b \) for the sum.
This forgets the sign flip, reporting \( b \) instead of \( -b \).
This computes the product \( r_1 r_2 \) directly instead of the sum.
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.

stem: Solve the initial value problem \( y'' + ([[b]])y' + ([[c]])y = 0 \), \( y(0) = [[y0]] \), \( y'(0) = [[v0]] \), and evaluate \( y([[t0]]) \).
solution: The characteristic roots are \( [[p]] \) and \( [[q]] \), so \( y = C_1 e^{[[p]]x} + C_2 e^{[[q]]x} \). The conditions give \( C_1 = [[c1]] \), \( C_2 = [[c2]] \), so \( y([[t0]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This swaps the two constants \( C_1 \) and \( C_2 \).
This splits the initial value equally between the modes, ignoring \( y'(0) \).
This keeps only the first exponential mode.
This keeps only the second exponential mode.
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.

stem: The characteristic equation of \( y'' + ([[b]])y' + ([[c]])y = 0 \) has a REPEATED root. Given \( y(0) = [[y0]] \) and \( y'(0) = [[v0]] \), evaluate \( y([[t0]]) \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to subtract \( ry_0 \) when solving for \( C_2 \), using \( v_0 \) directly.
This uses \( e^{-rx} \) instead of the correct \( e^{rx} \).
This drops the polynomial correction term entirely, as if the root were not repeated.
This multiplies the wrong constant by \( x \).
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.

stem: The characteristic equation of \( y'' + ([[c]])y = 0 \) has purely imaginary roots \( \pm [[w]]i \). Given \( y(0) = [[y0]] \) and \( y'(0) = [[v0]] \), evaluate \( y\!\left(\dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{2\cdot[[w]]}\right) \).
solution: With roots \( \pm [[w]]i \), \( y = y_0\cos([[w]]x) + \dfrac{v_0}{[[w]]}\sin([[w]]x) \). At \( x=\dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{2\cdot[[w]]} \), \( [[w]]x=\dfrac{[[k]]\pi}{2} \), so \( y = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This swaps the roles of sine and cosine.
This forgets to divide the velocity term by \( [[w]] \).
This uses the wrong sign on the sine term.
This drops the velocity term entirely.
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.

stem: The characteristic equation of \( y'' + ([[b]])y' + ([[c]])y = 0 \) has complex conjugate roots \( [[p]] \pm [[q]]i \). Given \( y(0) = [[y0]] \) and \( y'(0) = [[v0]] \), evaluate \( y\!\left(\dfrac{[[m]]\pi}{2\cdot[[q]]}\right) \).
solution: With roots \( [[p]]\pm[[q]]i \), \( y = e^{[[p]]x}\left(y_0\cos([[q]]x) + \dfrac{v_0-[[p]]y_0}{[[q]]}\sin([[q]]x)\right) \). At \( x=\dfrac{[[m]]\pi}{2\cdot[[q]]} \), \( [[q]]x=\dfrac{[[m]]\pi}{2} \), giving \( y = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the wrong sign in the exponential envelope, growing instead of decaying (or vice versa).
This swaps the roles of sine and cosine inside the oscillatory factor.
This forgets to subtract \( py_0 \) before dividing by \( q \) in the sine coefficient.
This drops the exponential envelope entirely.
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.
